docs.title: The Blight Veil

docs.title: How to use this site
docs.text:
• Use the left navigation to jump between sessions, factions, and player hooks.
  • Links are relative and will work when previewing locally with mkdocs serve or when the site is deployed.
  • If you plan to edit frequently, consider using Obsidian for fast linking and later exporting the vault into this docs/ folder for publishing.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text:
This repo contains the campaign notes and DM resources. To contribute:

  1. Edit the relevant Markdown file in docs/.
  2. Preview locally with mkdocs serve.
  3. Commit and push changes to your GitHub repository.

Generated index created by assistant to organize existing files.

docs.title: The Blighted
docs.text: Overview: The Blighted are those forever marked by the Veil’s corruption — beings caught between life and decay. Once human, now transformed by exposure to the Veil’s energy, they are shunned, hunted, or pitied. Their existence defies the natural order, yet among them burns a fierce will to survive and redefine what it means to be alive.

docs.title: Nature of the Blight
docs.text: The Blight is both curse and revelation. It mutates flesh and spirit, awakening strange abilities and warped perceptions. No two Blighted are alike: some channel impossible strength, others speak with unseen entities, and some simply waste away. The Concord brands all as heretics and monsters — but in the wastes, the Blighted call themselves the Next Dawn.

docs.title: The First Veilborn
docs.text:
A mythic figure — the first human to survive direct contact with the Veil. Said to be immortal, ageless, and suffused with Veil energy. The First Veilborn is less a ruler than a living symbol, guiding the Blighted through vision and prophecy.

Principles: Survival through adaptation; transcendence over purity. Allegiances: None publicly, though many whisper of secret pacts with rogue Concord scholars. Notable Events: The Dreaming Exodus; the Veilheart Accord.

docs.title: Esmir the Hollow
docs.text:
Once a Faith inquisitor, Esmir was Blighted in the field and left to die. Now, his hollowed form radiates pale light from within, and his followers see him as a messiah. He preaches that the Blight is evolution — the next stage of existence beyond flesh.

Principles: Transformation as salvation; rebellion as freedom. Allegiances: Allied loosely with radical Free Peoples leaders and Veil cults. Notable Events: The Siege of Wornspire; the massacre at Glassfall.

docs.title: The Veilborne Creed
docs.text: A growing belief among the Blighted that the Veil is not destruction, but awakening. They see themselves as envoys of a new world order, destined to inherit what remains when the pure consume themselves.

docs.title: Relations with Other Factions
docs.text:
• The Concord: Mortal enemies — hunted relentlessly as abominations.
  • The Faith: Objects of fear and fascination; heretics to most, divine to few.
  • The Free Peoples: Occasional allies of necessity, especially smugglers and outcasts.

docs.title: Notable Events
docs.text:
• The Siege of Wornspire: The Blighted, led by Esmir the Hollow, defended the ruined city of Wornspire against Concord forces. The battle ended in devastation, scattering survivors and fueling legends of Blighted resilience.
  • The Blighted Exodus: A mass migration of Blighted outcasts fleeing Concord purges, seeking sanctuary in the Veil-touched wilds. This event marked the birth of new enclaves and the rise of the Veilborne Creed.
  • The Fall of Lume: The city of Lume, once a beacon of knowledge, was consumed by the Veil after a failed experiment to harness its power. Many Blighted trace their origins to this catastrophe.
  • The Veilheart Accord: A secret pact among Blighted leaders to unite their scattered enclaves, share knowledge, and resist outside threats. The Accord remains shrouded in mystery, rumored to involve forbidden magics and prophecy.

docs.title: The Cindersong
docs.text: Overview: The Cindersong is a secretive network of musicians, rebels, and heretics who preserve forbidden hymns and lost cultural heritage. Their coded songs carry messages of hope, resistance, and hidden truths, echoing through the underbelly of Concord society. To the authorities, they are dangerous agitators; to the oppressed, they are a lifeline and a spark of rebellion.

docs.title: Origins & Beliefs
docs.text: Born from the ashes of the Godfall and Concord purges, the Cindersong formed to protect melodies and stories deemed heretical. They believe music and memory can heal the wounds of the Blight Veil and awaken the world to its true history. Their hymns are said to carry divine resonance, shifting reality and inspiring courage.

docs.title: Leadership & Structure
docs.text: The Cindersong is decentralized, led by a council of hidden conductors and influential performers. Notable members include: - Marla Veinwright: Defiant heretic, symbol of resistance, whose song sparked public unrest. - The Whisperer: An anonymous figure who coordinates coded messages and safehouses.

docs.title: Principles & Allegiances
docs.text:
• Preserve forbidden knowledge and culture.
  • Oppose Concord oppression and censorship.
  • Aid the Blighted, exiles, and other marginalized groups.
  • Forge alliances with Ashroot shamans, Free Cities traders, and sympathetic Concord officials.

docs.title: Notable Events
docs.text:
• The Choir of Ashes: A public performance that led to mass arrests and the spread of Cindersong influence.
  • The Rescue of Marla: A pivotal moment where the party may intervene, shaping the faction’s future.
  • Infiltration of Concord: Cindersong sympathizers have reached even the capital’s heart, as seen in session 1.

docs.title: Role in the Campaign
docs.text: The Cindersong offers quests, secret knowledge, and moral dilemmas. Their actions can sway public opinion, incite rebellion, or reveal hidden truths about the Blight Veil and the Concord’s history.

docs.title: The Concord
docs.text: Overview: The Concord is the dominant governing power of the known world — a disciplined and unyielding regime born from the ashes of chaos after the Veil descended. It is ruled by the Trifarix, a Council of Three embodying the Concord’s founding ideals: Might, Vision, and Guile. Together, they maintain a delicate balance of strength, intellect, and cunning that preserves civilization against the encroaching Blight.

docs.title: High Marshal Varric Thane — The Pillar of Might
docs.text:
Title: Warden of the Concordant Armies Principle: Strength through unity and conquest. Allegiance: The Legion of the Standard (the Concord’s standing army and colonial enforcers).

Profile: Varric Thane rose from a line of frontier-born soldiers — men and women hardened by endless skirmishes against the “heathen provinces.” A towering figure wrapped in ceremonial steel, he carries the Banner of the First Flame, a relic said to have burned away heresy during the founding wars.

To Thane, mercy is treason, and compromise is weakness. He believes that the Concord’s peace can only be maintained through overwhelming dominance — of body, of faith, and of will. His campaigns are brutal but efficient, his soldiers fanatically loyal.

Past Events: - Personally led the Siege of Aster’s Hollow, where he burned an entire city to stop the spread of “blight contagion.” - Rumored to have executed his own brother for questioning the Concord’s war on the southern tribes. - Recently began clashing with the Pillar of Vision over the “purity” of new conscripts. Thane insists that the Concord’s armies must accept any able-bodied recruit, regardless of background, so long as they prove loyalty and strength. He believes that survival demands pragmatism and numbers, even if it means conscripting those with questionable lineage or minor Veil-touched traits. Elira Voss, however, demands strict spiritual and genetic screening. She fears that allowing the impure—those with suspected heretical beliefs, blighted ancestry, or exposure to forbidden magics—will invite corruption into the ranks. Her clergy push for rigorous tests, rituals, and surveillance, rejecting anyone who fails to meet their standards of purity. Their conflict has led to heated debates, stalled recruitment drives, and growing tension between the military and the Church. Some soldiers whisper that Voss’s standards are impossible, while others fear Thane’s leniency will doom the Concord to internal rot.

Symbol: A broken sword reforged and bound with iron wire — representing discipline through suffering.

docs.title: Matriarch Elira Voss — The Pillar of Vision
docs.text:
Title: Keeper of the Veiled Truths Principle: Strength through enlightenment and divine order. Allegiance: The Church of the Veil, the Concord’s state faith.

Profile: Elira Voss is both revered and feared — a pale, unaging woman who claims to hear the whispers of saints lost beyond the Blight Veil. Once a scholar of natural philosophy, she turned zealot after a “revelation” during a pilgrimage where she vanished for three days in the ash wastes.

Now, she preaches that the Concord’s expansion is divinely ordained — that blight and heresy are tests meant to separate the pure from the false. Her temples double as schools and surveillance hubs; her clergy catalog “impure thoughts” as sins against the Concord’s unity.

Past Events:

  • Authored the Scriptures of the Veil, a doctrine that redefined blight as a “holy sickness.”

  • Accused of concealing miracles that contradict official Church teaching.

  • Known to hold secret midnight sessions with “dreamspeakers” — individuals believed to channel forbidden knowledge.

Symbol: A single eye, painted closed, weeping black wax.

docs.title: Lord Silven Corvane — The Pillar of Guile
docs.text:
Title: Chancellor of Coin and Whisper

Principles: Strength through cunning, information, and the unseen hand.

Allegiances: The Guild Compact — a network of spies, financiers, and merchant-lords who keep the Concord’s economy alive (and its people obedient).

Past Events: - Brokered the Treaty of Ashen Reach, ending a rebellion by selling out both rebel leaders and their loyalists. - Survived three assassination attempts — the latest by poisoned communion wine. - Secretly funds an underground “truth press” that publishes heretical pamphlets about the Trifarix.

Profile:

Silven Corvane presents himself as a charming, soft-spoken aristocrat — the kind who never dirties his hands but always gets his way. Beneath the velvet lies a spider’s patience and a serpent’s smile. His influence seeps through trade, debt, and rumor alike.

He views the Concord not as a moral crusade but as a marketplace of power. He funds both sides of the colonial wars, sells relics smuggled from forbidden ruins, and quietly removes those who might upset the “balance” — often through whispers rather than steel.

Symbol: A coiled serpent biting its tail, its scales made of coins.

docs.title: Philosophy and Law
docs.text: The Concord’s doctrine preaches that civilization must be preserved at any cost. The Concord Law ensures stability through rigid hierarchy, surveillance, and militarized order. Citizens are taught that unity is strength — dissent, heresy, and mutation are cancers to be purged.

docs.title: Orrenval
docs.text: Orrenval is the heart of the Concord, a city built atop the ruins of ancient temples and battlefields from the Godfall. Its origins lie in the unification of warring city-states, chosen for its strategic location and spiritual significance. Today, Orrenval is a sprawling metropolis of stone towers, bustling markets, and imposing statues of Concord heroes. The city is a center of governance, faith, and military power, where the Trifarix rules and the fate of the world is often decided.

docs.title: The Sanctara
docs.text: The Sanctara is Orrenval’s holy chapel, originally constructed as a shrine to the Dawnfather and later expanded into a grand cathedral after the Godfall. Its foundations are said to rest on sacred ground where the veil between worlds is thinnest. In the current day, the Sanctara serves as both a place of worship and a seat of spiritual authority for the Concord. Its halls are filled with relics, murals depicting the Godfall, and the ever-present scent of incense. The clergy of the Sanctara oversee rituals, trials, and the cataloging of miracles, making it a focal point for both faith and political intrigue.

docs.title: Relations with Other Factions
docs.text:
• The Faith: A strained alliance — the Concord uses religion as a tool of control but fears the Faith’s growing zealotry.
  • The Blighted: Treated as abominations; exterminated or enslaved under the Law of Purity.
  • The Free Peoples: Viewed as anarchists and smugglers who threaten the Concord’s monopoly on trade and truth.

docs.title: Notable Events
docs.text:
• Founding of the Trifarix after the Collapse: In the aftermath of the Godfall and the collapse of old orders, three leaders united to form the Trifarix, establishing the Concord’s ruling council and restoring stability to a fractured world.
  • The Codification of Concord Law: The Trifarix enacted a sweeping set of laws to enforce unity, suppress dissent, and regulate faith and magic. This legal code became the backbone of Concord society and its expansion.
  • The Purges of the First Blighted: In response to the rise of Veil-touched outcasts, the Concord launched brutal purges to cleanse its lands, driving survivors into exile and cementing the regime’s reputation for ruthless order.
  • The Reclamation of the Iron Marches: Concord armies retook the war-torn Iron Marches from rebel factions and blighted forces, securing vital trade routes and expanding the regime’s influence across the frontier.

docs.title: The Faith
docs.text: Overview: The Faith is the spiritual spine of the known world — a monolithic institution fractured by doubt. Its followers worship the Silent Pantheon, gods who turned mute after the Blight’s arrival. Once a force of unity, the Faith now teeters between redemption and fanaticism, each sect interpreting divine silence as either punishment or liberation.

docs.title: Doctrine and Belief
docs.text:
The Faith teaches that the Veil is the gods’ test — a divine crucible to burn away weakness. Its clergy preach that only through obedience, ritual, and sacrifice can the gods be stirred to speech once more. Yet cracks run deep: not all agree on what the silence means.

  • The Dawn Orthodoxy: Believes the gods’ silence is a test of faith, demanding purity and obedience.
  • The Ashen Veil: Teaches that humanity’s sins deafened the gods, and only through penance can the world be redeemed.
  • The Veilborn Heresy: Claims the Blighted are divine messengers, not curses.

docs.title: Archon Seraphine Vale — The Dawn Voice
docs.text:
A charismatic preacher and master orator, Seraphine leads the Faith’s largest orthodox branch. Her honeyed words conceal ruthless conviction. She preaches that faith is light — and all who cast shadow must be purged.

Principles: Purity through devotion; faith as the shield of civilization. Allegiances: Supports the Concord publicly, but sees herself as the true moral authority. Notable Events: Led the restoration of the Sunspire Basilica; personally sanctioned the execution of the Heretic Prophet.

docs.title: Mother Rhenna of the Ashen Veil
docs.text:
A gaunt, ascetic mystic who claims to have heard echoes of the gods within the Blight’s storms. Her followers scourge themselves in ritual repentance, wandering the wastelands to suffer on behalf of mankind.

Principles: Redemption through pain and silence. Allegiances: Secretly trades knowledge with the Blighted, believing their transformation is divine punishment. Notable Events: Founded the Order of the Ashen Path; witnessed the Burning of the Oracle Towers.

docs.title: The Inquisition of the Veil
docs.text: The Faith’s militant arm — inquisitors trained to identify heresy, mutation, and forbidden magic. They are both feared and respected, operating independently but sanctioned by the Concord when convenient.

docs.title: Relations with Other Factions
docs.text:
• The Concord: An uneasy partnership; the Faith provides moral legitimacy, the Concord offers protection.
  • The Blighted: Declared abominations, though some sects secretly seek to understand them.
  • The Free Peoples: Viewed as heathens — yet Faith missionaries often seek to convert them at the frontier.

docs.title: Notable Events
docs.text:
• The Burning of the Oracle Towers
  • The Schism of the Veilborn Heresy
  • The Rise of the Inquisitorial Orders
  • The Miracle of the Sunspire

docs.title: The Free Peoples
docs.text: Overview: The Free Peoples are a scattered confederation of exiles, nomads, smugglers, and survivors who refuse the yoke of the Concord. Bound by necessity rather than law, they move between the Veil’s scars and the remnants of fallen nations, living off trade, salvage, and sheer defiance.

docs.title: Culture and Ideals
docs.text: Freedom is their creed — but freedom, in this age, is brutal. The Free Peoples reject the Concord’s hierarchy and the Faith’s dogma, living instead by the rule of the road: no crown, no altar, no chains. They preserve songs, crafts, and stories lost to the Blight, carrying the memory of what the world once was.

docs.title: Captain Mirella “Ashhand” Coren
docs.text:
A smuggler queen and folk hero, Ashhand made her name running food, medicine, and forbidden knowledge through Veil-infested lands. Her burned hands — forever blackened by Blightfire — are a mark of courage and defiance.

Principles: Freedom through cunning; survival through loyalty. Allegiances: Trades with both Blighted enclaves and Faith pilgrims; maintains a tense peace with the Concord’s border guards. Notable Events: Brokered the Pact of the Free Winds; led the rescue of the Emberfield Convoy.

docs.title: The Wanderer’s Council
docs.text:
A loose circle of clan leaders, free captains, and elder traders who meet once each turning to decide on trade routes, truces, and codes of conduct. The Council’s authority is symbolic, but their decisions carry moral weight among the Free Peoples.

Principles: Community through choice, not coercion. Allegiances: Sometimes coordinates with sympathetic Concord defectors. Notable Events: Declared neutrality during the Silver Roads Blockade; outlawed Faith missionary encampments.

docs.title: Economy and Survival
docs.text: The Free Peoples thrive on adaptability. They scavenge, repurpose, and barter across a fragmented world. Their caravans are both marketplaces and fortresses, guarded by those who know no home but the horizon.

docs.title: Relations with Other Factions
docs.text:
• The Concord: Enemies in law, but indispensable traders in truth.
  • The Faith: Viewed with disdain; missionaries rarely survive long among them.
  • The Blighted: Allies of necessity, often offering sanctuary or safe passage.

docs.title: Notable Events
docs.text:
• The Pact of the Free Winds: A historic agreement among the Free Peoples, ensuring open trade and mutual aid between caravans and settlements, regardless of origin or creed.
  • The Burning of the Dusk Market: A tragic night when Concord agents set fire to a bustling market suspected of harboring heretics, forcing survivors to flee deeper into the wastes.
  • The Blockade of the Silver Roads: A tense standoff where Free Peoples caravans broke through a Concord military blockade, securing vital supplies for isolated communities.
  • The Fall of Wornspire Refugees: The collapse of the Wornspire enclave, sending waves of desperate refugees into Free Peoples territory and reshaping alliances across the frontier.
