docs.title: 🜂 Common Knowledge: The World Beneath the Blight Veil
1. The Concord
docs.text:
• The Concord is the ruling power over much of the known lands — a theocratic empire guided by the Trifarix, a council of three divine authorities said to speak for the gods themselves.
  • The Concord claims to bring order, light, and purity to the world — though some whisper that their wars are fought as much for faith as for dominion.
  • Its capital city, Orrenval, gleams with white stone and gold trim. At its heart stands the Sanctara, a towering chapel where the Trifarix hold court.
  • The Concord’s influence reaches far, but its control thins at the fringes, where rumors of heresy, witchcraft, and the Veil’s corruption spread unchecked.

docs.title: 2. The Blight Veil
docs.text:
• The Blight Veil is not merely a phenomenon — it’s a scar across the horizon. A roiling, silvery mist that swallows the land, warps nature, and devours reason.
  • People do not enter the Veil. Those who do either vanish entirely or return… changed.
  • No one knows its true origin, though Concord doctrine insists it is divine punishment — a consequence of ancient sin.
  • The Veil moves. Slowly, silently, but undeniably — encroaching year by year. Entire provinces have been “Veiled” and lost to history.

docs.title: 3. Faith and Doctrine
docs.text:
• The Concord worships the Radiant Triune, a unity of three divine aspects: Judgment, Mercy, and Order.
  • Heresy, witchcraft, or unsanctioned miracles are punishable by death or purging.
  • Holy banners, sermons, and relics are everywhere. Citizens are expected to attend sermons, tithe to the Sanctara, and report signs of corruption.
  • The Inquisitors of the Concord are both feared and revered — they are said to see the stain of the Veil upon any soul.

docs.title: 4. The Current Era
docs.text:
• It is the Year of Saint Velan’s Eclipse, an omen of unrest. Strange lights have appeared along the Veil’s edge.
  • Refugees from border towns speak of voices in the mist and rivers that run backwards.
  • Orrenval is under growing tension: shortages, protests, and whispers that the Trifarix no longer agree behind closed doors.
  • Still, the people cling to the belief that the Concord — and faith — will save them.

docs.title: 5. Daily Life Under the Concord
docs.text:
• Every town has a chapel, a warden, and a record of purity for each family. To live without one is to invite suspicion.
  • Purity checks are common: priests and wardens travel to test citizens for signs of corruption — discoloration, unnatural dreams, unholy whispers.
  • Many wear charms or relics (blessed rings, silver-threaded cloth, prayer etchings) to ward off the Veil.
  • Trade and travel are tightly monitored; leaving one’s assigned district requires written sanction from the Concord.

docs.title: 6. The Veiled Terrors (Rumors)
docs.text:
• Children are taught cautionary tales: The Hollow Saints, The Silver-Eyed, The Blightborne.
  • Some soldiers claim to have seen living shadows and ashen figures beyond the mist — remnants of the fallen world.
  • It is said that magic used too freely draws the Veil’s attention. “Unbound power invites unbound ruin.”

docs.title: 7. Known Factions and Powers
docs.text:
• The Trifarix – the ruling three, each representing a face of the Triune faith.
  • The Concord Guard – the empire’s disciplined military.
  • The Inquisition – the purging arm of the faith, ferreting out corruption.
  • The Warden Orders – regional enforcers maintaining local order.
  • The Cartographers of the Pale – secretive explorers who map the creeping edge of the Veil.
  • The Heretic Chorus (rumored) – those who reject the Triune and claim the Veil hides a forgotten truth.

docs.title: 8. Cultural Memory
docs.text:
• Everyone has grown up under the shadow of sermons warning of the Veil’s hunger.
  • No one alive remembers a world without it. The Veil is as natural — and as feared — as death itself.
  • Festivals still exist — bright, fervent, desperate attempts to hold onto normalcy — but joy feels like defiance these days.

docs.title: Mythologies & Legends
docs.text:
• The Godscar: A forbidden region said to be the resting place of slain gods. No one returns unchanged.
  • The Song of the Lost: A tale of a bard who wandered the Veil and returned with a melody that can heal or destroy.

docs.title: Magical Phenomena
docs.text:
• Veil Anomalies: Places where time loops, dreams become real, or the dead speak. Some are mapped, most are feared.
  • Legendary Artifacts:
  • The Lantern of Dawn: Said to reveal truth and banish illusions.
  • Ashroot Seed: A relic that can restore corrupted land.

docs.title: Unresolved Mysteries
docs.text:
• What secrets do the Veilward Obelisks truly hold?
  • Who was the First Veilborn, and what became of them?
  • Why do some regions resist the Veil while others succumb?
  • What happened to the first Concord expedition into the Godscar?
  • Are the Cindersong’s melodies truly magical, or just dangerous ideas?

docs.title: Timeline of Major Events
docs.text:
• The Godfall: The war that shattered the divine and birthed the Veil.
  • Founding of the Concord: Unification under the Trifarix.
  • Ashroot Rebellion: The last stand of the forest shamans.
  • The Blighted Exodus: Survivors fleeing the Veil’s advance.
  • The Merchant’s Pact: Free Cities unite to resist Concord control.

docs.title: The Godfall: Birth of the Blight Veil & The Concord
The World Before the Godfall
docs.text: Before the war, the world was a tapestry of vibrant cultures, city-states, and wild frontiers. Gods walked among mortals, their temples rising above bustling markets and sacred groves. Magic was abundant, woven into daily life—healers, seers, and artisans all drew upon divine gifts. Politics revolved around alliances between priesthoods, merchant guilds, and noble houses. Festivals honored the pantheon, and disputes were settled by ritual or council.

docs.title: Instigating Events
docs.text:
• The Shattering of the Dawnfather’s Pact: A powerful god’s promise of peace was broken by mortal ambition, leading to jealousy and strife among the divine.
  • The Rise of Heretical Cults: Secret societies began to worship forbidden powers, undermining the old order.
  • Assassination of the High Oracle: The murder of a beloved spiritual leader shattered trust between factions.
  • The Theft of the Godseed: A relic capable of granting godhood was stolen, sparking a race for power.

docs.title: Major Players
docs.text:
• The Concord: A coalition of city-states and priesthoods seeking order and unity.
  • The Ashroot Tribes: Guardians of ancient nature magic, resisting Concord expansion.
  • The Free Peoples: Nomads, traders, and rebels caught between warring powers.
  • The Pantheon: Gods of light, shadow, nature, and fate—some noble, some vengeful.
  • The Godscar Cults: Fanatics who sought to harness divine death for their own ends.
  • Heroes & Villains:
    • Varric Thane: Concord general, later the first Lord Commander.
    • Matriarch Elira Voss: Visionary priestess, survivor of the Godfall.
    • Ashhand Coren: Smuggler queen, broker of forbidden alliances.

docs.title: Notable Battles & Turning Points
docs.text:
• The Siege of Lume: Concord forces besieged the city of Lume, shattering its guardian deity and unleashing the first Veil.
  • The Night of Falling Stars: Multiple gods were slain in a single night, their deaths tearing rifts in reality.
  • The Burning of Ashroot: Concord pyres swept through sacred forests, driving the tribes into exile.
  • The Battle of the Godscar: Cultists attempted to resurrect a dead god, resulting in a catastrophic explosion of Veil energy.

docs.title: Consequences of the Godfall
docs.text:
• Creation of the Blight Veil: The deaths of gods tore open the fabric of reality, releasing the Blight Veil—a shimmering haze that warps magic, time, and life itself.
  • Rise of the Concord: The Concord emerged as the dominant power, enforcing order through martial law, faith, and propaganda.
  • Transformation of Magic: Divine magic became unstable; new forms of Veil-touched sorcery appeared.
  • Societal Upheaval: Old priesthoods fell, new cults rose, and survivors struggled to adapt to a world haunted by divine loss.

docs.title: Lasting Scars & Mysteries
docs.text:
• The Godscar: A region where reality remains broken, haunted by echoes of divine agony.
  • The First Veilborn: Legends speak of mortals transformed by direct contact with the Veil—some revered, others hunted.
  • Lost Relics: Artifacts from the war, such as the Godseed and the Lantern of Dawn, remain hidden or fiercely guarded.
  • Unanswered Questions:
    • What truly happened to the slain gods?
    • Can the Blight Veil be healed, or is it the world’s new destiny?
    • Who benefits from the chaos that followed?

docs.title: 🕯️ 1. The Etherlight Concord
docs.text: Core Themes: Theocratic control, moral purges, elite hypocrisy

docs.title: Overview:
docs.text: The spiritual and political heart of the empire. Gleaming cities, propaganda-filled sermons, and inquisitorial spies in every crowd. “Purity” is law, and “heresy” is defined by whoever holds the most power.

docs.title: Quest: "The Choir of Ashes"
docs.text:
Start: A child sings a forbidden melody in a plaza. The crowd panics. Inquisitors seize the child. Investigation: The players learn the tune is from an old hymn that predates the Concord but is now banned as heretical. Following leads to a hidden network of musicians called the Cindersong, who preserve lost cultural heritage through coded songs.

Choices:

  • Join the Cindersong and help them spread their message through underground performances.
  • Expose them to gain favor with the Concord — but find the inquisitors using the lyrics for torture.
  • Reveal the truth to the public: the song is Concord's own rewritten history, purged for political reasons.

docs.title: 🌿 2. Ashroot
docs.text: Core Themes: Cultural resilience, forbidden rites, nature vs. civilization

docs.title: Overview:
docs.text: Dense forests, sacred groves, and hidden villages where ancestral rites persist. Viewed by the Concord as "uncivilized" and "corrupted." A land of secret pacts and blood-stained memory.

docs.title: Quest: "The Wound in the Wood"
docs.text:
Start: A tree in a sacred grove bleeds real blood after a Concord missionary desecrates it. Spirits grow restless. Locals blame each other. Investigation: The tree was a seal binding an old nature-spirit, wounded by colonial magic. A warband of Ashroot shamans wants to awaken it to fight back.

Choices:

  • Help awaken the spirit and unleash it — potentially powerful but uncontrollable.
  • Rebind it, preserving balance but leaving the land vulnerable to more Concord raids.
  • Seek a third path — use arcane knowledge or diplomacy to heal the seal and forge a new pact.

docs.title: ⛰️ 3. The Godscar
docs.text: Core Themes: War trauma, blighted landscapes, broken divinity

docs.title: Overview:
docs.text: Once a holy land — now shattered by divine warfare and forbidden experiments. The soil is cursed, the air buzzes with residual divine energy. Ruins of lost civilizations dot the region.

docs.title: Quest: "Shadows of the Shell-God"
docs.text:
Start: A refugee camp is being haunted by visions of a massive, limbless divine corpse buried beneath the hills. Investigation: The players can follow trails to a massive crater where a god once fell. Artifacts nearby grant miracles — or madness.

Choices:

  • Harvest godflesh for powerful but unstable magical items.
  • Try to cleanse the land, but doing so destroys the artifacts — and may awaken other dangerous remnants.
  • Offer the site to the Godscar cult, who want to restore divine war through sacrifice.

docs.title: 🌊 4. Ibaros & Corsaire Sound
docs.text: Core Themes: Rebellion, pirate democracy, Concord subterfuge

docs.title: Overview:
docs.text: An archipelago that resisted colonization the longest. Now it's a pirate haven, trade hub, and a brewing storm of political upheaval. Smugglers ferry forbidden texts and stolen relics.

docs.title: Quest: "The Salt-Born Pact"
docs.text:
Start: A pirate queen offers the players a rare artifact if they sabotage a Concord naval blockade. Investigation: The artifact is a relic of the sea god once worshipped across the islands — now outlawed. Her crew is becoming fanatical, influenced by dreams from the deep.

Choices:

  • Aid the blockade sabotage, empowering a rebellion that could spiral into religious fanaticism.
  • Steal the artifact and sell it — but suffer consequences as the sea god marks them.
  • Warn the Concord, but discover they’re conducting rituals against the same god to control weather.

docs.title: 🔥 5. Zathira
docs.text: Core Themes: Cultural melting pot, espionage, buried magic

docs.title: Overview:
docs.text: A frontier city-state recently absorbed by the Concord but still full of independent thinkers, rogue mages, and foreign traders. A powder keg of ideological tension.

docs.title: Quest: "The Mask of Three Faces"
docs.text:
Start: A Concord official is assassinated. The killers leave behind an ancient mask marked with three interlocking symbols. Investigation: The mask is tied to an old trinity cult (mind, flesh, and will) that once ruled Zathira in secret. Their surviving agents are plotting a return.

Choices:

  • Infiltrate the cult and take control of its network.
  • Expose it to the Concord — who decide to raze a whole district in retaliation.
  • Play both sides, manipulating Concord paranoia and cult ambitions for the players’ own ends.

docs.title: 🩸 6. The Riven Marches
docs.text: Core Themes: Border conflict, scorched earth, local resistance

docs.title: Overview:
docs.text: The contested buffer between Ashroot and the Concord. War-torn villages, famine, and shifting allegiances. Every faction has spies here.

docs.title: Quest: "Ashes for Bread"
docs.text:
Start: A village pleads for food aid from both Concord forces and Ashroot guerrillas. Each offers safety in exchange for loyalty. Investigation: The land is dying because a Concord general cursed the soil to force surrender years ago — the effects persist.

Choices:

  • Side with Concord, restore supplies, but enforce harsh loyalty and convert villagers.
  • Aid Ashroot fighters, but risk reprisals and executions.
  • Use forgotten nature magic to purify the land, turning the village into a neutral sanctuary — if the players can protect it.

docs.title: Rumor Mechanic: Luck Roll After Long Rest
docs.text: After each long rest, every player rolls a d20 Luck Roll: - 1–10: A bad rumor is spread about the character. - 11–20: A good rumor is spread about the character.

docs.title: Determining the Rumor
docs.text: The DM creates a rumor based on recent party actions, location, and NPCs present. Rumors can affect: - NPC attitudes (hostile, friendly, suspicious) - Quest opportunities (doors open or close) - Obstacles (guards, prices, access)

docs.title: Modifying the Luck Roll
docs.text: The DM may grant advantage/disadvantage or modify the roll by ±2 to ±5 based on: - Helping citizens, performing heroic deeds (+) - Causing trouble, breaking laws (–) - Using magic openly (±, depending on local views) - Spending resources to bribe, charm, or influence (+) - Failing or succeeding at major skill checks (±)

docs.title: Example Good Rumors
docs.text:
• “Did you hear? The party saved a child from the burning market!”
  • “They say those travelers healed the sick for free.”
  • “Word is, the newcomers are blessed by the Dawnfather.”

docs.title: Example Bad Rumors
docs.text:
• “I heard they stole from the merchant’s stall.”
  • “Those outsiders consort with heretics and blighted.”
  • “Someone saw them using forbidden magic in the square.”

docs.title: Adapting the Mechanic
docs.text:
• Use group rolls for party-wide reputation shifts.
  • Adjust rumor impact for major story events.
  • Allow players to spend Inspiration or gold to reroll or influence rumors.

This mechanic keeps the party’s reputation dynamic and responsive to their choices, adding social consequences and opportunities throughout the campaign.

docs.title: 🌍 Hometown & Placement in the World
docs.text:
• Hometown: Emberhold, a mining and smithing settlement built along the slopes of the volcanic ridge Ashenpeak in western Cindralith. Emberhold is known for its master smiths who work with rare ores tempered in volcanic forges. Many famous weapons and Concord relics have passed through its fires.
  • Temple of Radiant Dawn: Located just outside Emberhold, the monastery perches atop the cliffs, overlooking molten flows below. This temple is both sanctuary and forge-shrine — its priests believe that every spark is a reflection of Lathander’s dawn.

docs.title: 🔥 Major Events Tied to Backstory
docs.text:
1. The Scorching Procession When Fegulous was 10, the town of Emberhold experienced a strange omen: during the annual forging festival, the sky turned crimson, and fire genasi children (rare as they are) all displayed sparks of uncontrolled flame. Some whispered this was tied to the Blight Veil’s slow corruption, others that it was Lathander’s blessing. Fergie’s emotional flares during this event made them both feared and revered locally. Tie-in: The Concord keeps records of these omens — meaning Fegulous’s powers may already be known to them.

  2. The Veil-Touched Pilgrim Years later, while at the Temple, Fegulous tended to a wounded traveler who had wandered from the edges of the Blight Veil. This traveler, half-broken but full of strange tales, whispered of a “song of fire buried beneath the ashlands”. They mentioned a fire genasi scholar in the Concord capital, one who “hears the same fire you do.” Tie-in: This is what set Fergie on the path to the capital — and the PCs may later discover the traveler was a Cindersong sympathizer.

docs.title: 💎 The Porter-Stone
docs.text:
• Name: The Ember’s Heart (Black Opal).
  • Lore: Every youth in Emberhold undergoes the Choosing of the Stone. It is believed the stones are fragments of a primordial forge where the gods once shaped the world. Fergie’s black opal is particularly rare; miners whisper it comes from beneath Ashenpeak, where flame and shadow mingle.
  • Symbolic Significance: In Emberhold, black opals are tied to prophecy — it is said a black opal-bearer will either ignite a new dawn or burn the world to ash.

  • Possible Magical Property (low-level, grows with story):

  • Dormant: While carried, the stone glows faintly when near Blight Veil corruption (like a mini Geiger counter).

  • Awakened (later): Once per long rest, as a reaction, Fergie can reroll a failed saving throw against fire or radiant damage.
  • Fully Awakened (major arc): Grants resistance to necrotic damage when within the Blight Veil.

docs.title: 🎭 Campaign Integration
docs.text:
• Session 1 Hook: Marla (the heretic on trial) hums a fragment of the Cindersong that causes Fergie’s opal to pulse with warmth. It is the first time the stone has reacted to anything outside Emberhold.
  • Cindersong Connection: The Cindersong believes the black opal is a “key-stone” to unlocking their Hymn of Fire — an artifact or ritual weapon against the Concord. This will put Fergie squarely in the middle of both factions.
  • Concord Connection: Inquisitor Rellis has seen Fergie’s name in Concord records from Emberhold’s omens — he may recognize the stone, and suspect that Fergie is far more than “just another cleric.”

docs.title: From Paul
docs.text:
I was born to a long line of blacksmiths. I, unlike my family though, was born with supernatural fire powers. My father was an honest hard-working man, but felt it would be better for my future that I be raised outside of the Blacksmith ‘circuit’ - he left me to be brought up and educated in a monastery outside of town - the Temple of Radiant Dawn - from childhood. 

This sanctuary was devoted to the healing light of the Morninglord, Lathander. The temple’s priests taught me that life is the most sacred flame, and that fire was not only about destruction, but about life and rebirth. Over time, my relationship with fire deepened - my fire powers manifested themselves in line with how I was feeling. The priests encouraged his devotion, but whispered of his strange gift: whenever my emotions surged, so too did fire flicker at my fingertips. Anger singed wood. Joy lit candles without touch. Sadness left trails of smoke. I embraced these powers, and made a promise to myself that when my life would take me beyond my temple’s walls, I would learn to develop them and learn more about their nature. 

My special item is my “porter-stone”: a stone that town’s people choose at the age of 16 (really, it chooses you) in a coming of age ritual. Mine is a black opal, which in my town’s lore is said to have magical properties. Like many others in my culture, all my jewelry has attachments where I can place the stone - usually to my ring, but also my earrings, necklaces, anklet...

After years of service in my Temple - doing tasks like changing bandages, carrying water, comforting the sick. And learning the foundations of worshipping Life and servitude as a cleric, I’ve decided to leave my Temple for the Concord, where a wayward traveller that I once helped told me of an old Fire Genasi scholar that I could perhaps learn from or ask questions.

docs.title: 🌲 Hometown & Placement in the World
docs.text:
• Hometown: Bramblethane, a modest dwarven settlement tucked into the Auburnwilds (southern rim of the Ashenwood). Known for its ochre-red forests and rolling hills, it’s a place of good ale, middling craftsmanship, and a reputation for being “just fine.”
  • The Thunderwood clan is remembered in dwarven histories as legendary warrior-smiths who once wielded storm-forged weapons against giants in the Godfall era. These days, their legacy has dwindled to little more than family stories, woodcutting, and mediocre brawling tournaments.

docs.title: ⚡ Major Events Tied to Backstory
docs.text:
1. The Fall of the Thunder Arena Centuries ago, the Thunderwoods maintained an arena-temple devoted to Kord, god of storms and strength. The site was struck by a freak lightning storm during the Godfall, toppling its great pillars and burying it beneath the Auburnwild hills. Local lore claims that the Thunderwood legacy slumbers there until a true heir awakens it. Tie-in: Hog’s trinket (the iron tile) is a fragment of the temple’s shattered altar. His family passed it down, never knowing it was more than just a memento.

  2. The Bottomless Fight Hog’s most infamous fight — the trousers incident — somehow made its way into Concord gossip, bards’ tavern songs, and even city posters (“See Hog the Bottomless! He Fights! He Falls! He Smiles Again!”). Though humiliating, this notoriety means Hog is already known when he arrives at the Concord capital. Some might sneer, but others (especially underground fighters and the Cindersong) respect his refusal to quit.

docs.title: ⚡ The Trinket: Iron Tile of the Gauntlet
docs.text:
• Name: Stormfist Fragment.
  • Appearance: A rough palm-sized iron tile etched with a gauntlet clenched in lightning. The design hums faintly when storms gather.
  • Lore: This was once part of the altar at the Thunder Arena, consecrated to Kord. The shattering of the altar scattered fragments across Thunderwood bloodlines, said to act as a “spark” waiting to reignite the clan’s storm.
  • Symbolic Significance: To Hog’s family, it’s just a keepsake. But to storm priests and Concord archivists, it’s a dangerous relic that could awaken elemental storms.

  • Magical Properties (scales with campaign):

  • Dormant: Hog can cause harmless static sparks to crackle around his fists when concentrating. Once per long rest, he can add 1d4 lightning damage to an unarmed strike.

  • Awakened (later): Hog can reroll a missed unarmed attack once per long rest, as lightning arcs guide his strike.
  • Fully Awakened (major arc): May channel Stormstrike — adding 2d6 lightning damage to all unarmed strikes for 1 minute (once per long rest).

docs.title: 🎭 Campaign Integration
docs.text:
• Session 1 Hook: As the Concord prepares to burn Marla, storm clouds gather unnaturally fast. Hog’s iron tile hums and grows warm, as though the gauntlet carved upon it is clenching harder. If Hog holds it during the scene, faint static crackles around his knuckles.
  • Concord Connection: Some inquisitors recognize the gauntlet mark — archived as a forbidden symbol of pre-Concord storm cults. Hog could be targeted as a potential heretic, even if he doesn’t understand why.
  • Cindersong Connection: Members of the Cindersong see Hog’s resilience as proof he embodies the song of storms. They may try to recruit him as a folk hero, seeing him as the living spark of Thunderwood legend.

docs.title: 🌀 Narrative Potential
docs.text:
• Underdog Rising: Hog’s laughable fighting record becomes mythic — the “Bottomless” dwarf who just won’t stay down. NPCs (and players) will love him for it.
  • Prophecy of the Thunderwood: The Blight Veil itself reacts violently to storms; Hog’s stormline blood may hold the key to shaping or resisting that corruption.
  • Crossroads of Reputation: Does Hog embrace his role as a “joke turned legend” or strive to prove the Thunderwood name truly belongs among heroes?

docs.title: Eris’ Amulet
docs.text:
(Also known in whispers as the Widow’s Sigil)

Wondrous Item, rare (requires attunement by a Fighter)

Forged of silver and bone, the amulet bears an etched spiral symbol — the same mark once worn by his wife Eris, a witch of the Veil. It pulses faintly with warmth when he dreams, whispering her voice through the static of memory. Though he believes it cursed, it grants him uncanny resilience in battle — a gift, or perhaps a leash.

docs.title: 🩸 Stage I — Dormant (“The Weight of Memory”)
docs.text:
Unlocked upon attunement.

The amulet feeds on guilt and blood. Its magic remains faint, flickering like a dying candle.

Properties:

  • Echo of Her Voice. When reduced to 0 HP but not killed outright, you automatically succeed your first death saving throw. The amulet lets out a faint whisper — your wife’s voice calling your name.
  • Relentless Surge. Once per long rest, when you reduce a creature to 0 HP, you regain 1d10 + your Constitution modifier HP.
  • Cursed Bond. When you first attune to the amulet, you gain recurring nightmares of Eris and your lost daughter. You cannot willingly unattune from it until the curse is broken or transformed.

Flavour: At this stage, the amulet feels like both protection and punishment. It keeps him alive when he would rather die, binding him to a promise unfinished.

docs.title: ⚔️ Stage II — Awakened (“The Witch’s Promise”)
docs.text:
Unlocked after a major confrontation with remnants of the Dawnshield, or discovering evidence of Eris’ survival.

The amulet awakens, its spiral mark glowing faintly crimson. It hums during combat, almost anticipating your strikes. The witch’s power entwines with your martial instinct.

Properties:

  • All previous effects, plus:
  • Veilstrike. Once per short or long rest, when you use Action Surge, your weapon attacks deal an additional 1d8 necrotic damage until the end of your turn. Each creature damaged this way must succeed on a DC 15 Wisdom saving throw or be frightened of you until the start of your next turn.
  • Witch’s Resilience. When you take damage that would reduce you below half HP, you can use your reaction to gain temporary HP equal to your Fighter level. When you do, your eyes briefly glow with the same hue as the amulet.

Curse Deepens: The amulet begins to whisper mid-battle — sometimes with tactical clarity, sometimes with haunting phrases (“She’s waiting.” “They burned her.” “You left us.”). Failing a DC 13 Wisdom save after each long rest may impose one level of exhaustion as nightmares bleed into waking thought.

Flavour: He begins to fight with supernatural efficiency — movements guided by memory and magic both. But it becomes harder to tell whether he’s drawing strength from his will, or from the thing that lives in the amulet.

docs.title: 🔥 Stage III — Fully Awakened / Transformed (“The Breaking of the Bond”)
docs.text:
Unlocked upon completing the character’s major arc — when he either destroys or transforms the amulet (his choice). This marks the resolution of his personal story.

At this point, the amulet’s power either becomes cleansed (if he chooses redemption) or corrupted (if he chooses vengeance).

You can choose one of the following outcomes:

docs.title: ✨ Path of Redemption — “The Cleansed Sigil”
docs.text:
The amulet’s spiral cracks open, light pouring through the fissures. The whispers cease. He learns to forgive — not Eris, perhaps, but himself.

Properties:

  • You gain advantage on death saving throws.
  • Purged Surge. When you use Second Wind, all allies within 10 feet regain HP equal to your Fighter level.
  • Soul Untethered. You can no longer be frightened or charmed. The amulet becomes inert, a symbol rather than a source of power.

Flavour: The amulet is no longer a prison but a memento. It no longer glows or hums — only warms faintly when he speaks of his daughter.

docs.title: 💀 Path of Vengeance — “The Widow’s Heart”
docs.text:
Instead of breaking the amulet, he merges with it — accepting the witch’s curse as part of himself. His body bears faint runic scars where the chain once rested.

Properties:

  • All previous Awakened effects, plus:
  • Vengeful Spirit. When you reduce a creature to 0 HP, you can immediately move up to half your movement and make another weapon attack (no action required).
  • Death Denied. Once per long rest, when you would be reduced to 0 HP, you instead drop to 1 HP and deal an additional 2d8 necrotic damage with your next attack.
  • Aura of Dread. Enemies within 10 feet who start their turn must make a DC 16 Wisdom saving throw or be frightened until the start of their next turn.

Flavour: The amulet fuses with his flesh, its spiral etched across his collarbone. His eyes burn red in combat; his voice carries the echo of another. Whether it’s Eris or something darker, no one can tell.

docs.title: Narrative Function
docs.text:
The amulet acts as the axis of his entire character arc:

  • Dormant: It symbolizes his grief and guilt.
  • Awakened: It merges his martial and magical instincts, forcing him to confront what’s left of Eris’ influence.
  • Transformed: It embodies his choice — redemption or corruption.

In gameplay, it rewards survival, aggression, and the thematic tension of his story: he’s too stubborn to die, too broken to rest, and too dangerous to ignore.

docs.title: Eris’ Amulet (Revised Lore)
docs.text: Also called: The Witch’s Hook, The Bone-Tether, The Chain of the Widow (Requires attunement by a Fighter)

docs.title: 💀 Why He Wears It
docs.text:
He doesn’t wear it out of love. He wears it because he can’t take it off.

When he found the ruins of their home — after the night of fire and blood — he discovered this amulet hanging amid the ashes, still warm to the touch. Out of fury and grief, he tried to smash it… and when he did, its chain melted into his skin.

Since then, he’s felt her presence — not a loving whisper, but a taunting echo. It pulls him toward places where she’s been, where witches gather, where the Blight leaks through the world. Whether this is a curse or some perverse tether she left behind, he doesn’t know — but it’s the only link he has to her and their child.

So he wears it for one reason:

“If that bitch is still out there, this damned thing will lead me to her.”

He hates it. He resents it. But he needs it. It’s both a leash and a compass.

docs.title: 🩸 Stage I — Dormant (“The Hook in the Heart”)
docs.text:
Unlocked upon attunement (cannot be removed once worn).

The amulet is inert, but it feels — it tightens slightly around his neck when magic or witchcraft is nearby.

Properties:

  • Hunter’s Pull. You can sense when a creature within 60 ft. casts a spell or uses a magical ability; the amulet tugs faintly in their direction.
  • Relentless Survival. When you are reduced to 0 HP but not killed outright, the amulet jolts you with necrotic energy. You instead drop to 1 HP and take 1 level of exhaustion. Once per long rest.
  • Cursed Attachment. You cannot remove or unattune from the amulet. Attempts to do so cause you to take 2d10 psychic damage and hear a whisper that says, “You’re not done yet.”

Flavour: The amulet is like a parasite. It keeps him alive against his will, driven by vengeance and obsession. He wears it because breaking it might kill him — or worse, sever his last hope of finding the truth.

docs.title: ⚔️ Stage II — Awakened (“The Witch’s Snare”)
docs.text:
Unlocked after encountering witches of the Blight or receiving a vision of Eris through the amulet.

The amulet’s influence grows stronger — its bone veins pulse faintly. It begins whispering in battle, feeding on rage.

Properties:

  • All previous effects, plus:
  • Veilstrike. Once per short or long rest, when you use Action Surge, your weapon attacks deal an additional 1d8 necrotic damage until the end of your turn.
  • Blood Compass. As an action, you can concentrate for 1 minute to learn the direction (not distance) of the nearest source of witchcraft or blight magic within 5 miles. Once per long rest.
  • Resentful Resilience. When you take damage that would reduce you below half HP, the amulet feeds on your pain: gain temporary HP equal to your Fighter level.

Curse Deepens: He begins to see her in his periphery — flickers of her form in smoke or flame. Sometimes she taunts him, sometimes pleads. Sometimes, her voice sounds like his daughter’s instead. He drinks to silence them.

Flavour: The amulet doesn’t just keep him alive — it keeps him angry. It wants him fighting, killing, feeding it with bloodshed. Whether Eris’ spirit drives it or something else does, it’s using his hatred as fuel.

docs.title: 🔥 Stage III — Fully Awakened (“The Breaking of the Hook”)
docs.text:
Unlocked after the major arc — when he chooses what to do with the amulet.

This is the moment of decision: Does he break the tether and free himself (perhaps at the cost of his life or connection to his daughter)? Or does he embrace it, letting its corruption become part of him?

docs.title: ✨ Path of Severance — “The Broken Hook”
docs.text:
He finally destroys the amulet — whether through witchfire, divine cleansing, or sheer will. The act leaves a black scar around his neck and a gaping silence where the whispers once were.

Properties:

  • The curse ends; the amulet crumbles into ash.
  • Permanently gain +1 to Constitution and resistance to necrotic damage.
  • Once per long rest, when reduced to 0 HP, you can instead drop to 1 HP — your will alone keeping you alive. (No exhaustion penalty.)

Flavour: The Blight’s whispers are gone. The world feels quieter — heavier. He’s finally free, but at a cost: the faint warmth that once hinted at his daughter’s soul is gone too.

docs.title: 💀 Path of Embrace — “The Witch’s Blade”
docs.text:
He chooses not to break it. Instead, he drives its chain into his wrist or chest, merging it with his body. The whispers stop — because now, they are his thoughts.

Properties:

  • You gain all Awakened effects.
  • Blighted Fury. When you reduce a creature to 0 HP, you can make another attack immediately (no action required).
  • Unholy Endurance. Once per long rest, when reduced to 0 HP, you instead regain 1 HP and gain temporary HP equal to your Fighter level + Constitution modifier.
  • Aura of Fear. Enemies within 10 ft. have disadvantage on Wisdom saving throws while you’re conscious.

Flavour: The amulet fuses with his veins, its bone etching crawling up his neck. His eyes glow faintly when he fights. He’s no longer just wearing the curse — he is it.

docs.title: 🕯️ Summary of Motivations & Symbolism
docs.text: Stage Theme Why He Keeps It What It Represents Dormant Guilt & Survival Can’t remove it; it’s his only lead to his wife/child The chain of obsession Awakened Rage & Obsession Uses it as a compass to hunt her and witches Hatred turned into purpose Fully Awakened Choice & Identity Decides if he’ll break free or become the monster Closure through destruction or acceptance

docs.title: 💭 Character Line Example
docs.text: “I don’t wear it for her. I wear it because it burns when she’s near. And one day, I’m going to follow that burn straight to her throat.”

docs.title: Character Integration: “The Broken Knight”
docs.text: Name: (TBD by player) Race/Class: (TBD — likely martial, fighter or paladin archetype) Alignment: Chaotic Neutral (once Lawful Good) Tone: Gritty, disillusioned, tragic

docs.title: Early Life: The Gutter-Son of Orrenval
docs.text:
He was born in Orrenval, the gilded capital of the Concord, though he saw none of its splendor. His earliest memories were of the city’s underbelly — the Canal Warrens, where the run-off from marble streets flows through tunnels of sewage and shadow. There, he fought to survive among other gutter-children.

Street brawls became his currency — fists for food, bruises for bedding. His knack for violence caught the attention of the Order of the Dawnshield, a militant holy order sworn to “preserve the light of the Concord.” The Dawnshield knights were seen as paragons of faith and law — but in truth, they were the Trifarix’s iron hand, enforcing Concord rule through fear and purges.

docs.title: The Knight of False Light
docs.text:
He rose swiftly through their ranks — a weapon made loyal through gratitude. They taught him obedience, devotion, and righteousness, but only insofar as it made him useful.

For years, he fought under the Dawnshield banner, leading crusades into the outlying provinces, stamping out “heresy.” His missions grew increasingly cruel: burning villages accused of witchcraft, executing “blighted” dissidents, and purging those who spoke against the Sanctara’s authority. He told himself it was for the greater good — protecting the innocent from the corruption of the Veil.

But his faith began to crack when he met Eris, a healer from a border settlement razed during one of his campaigns. She saw through his armor, both literal and moral. She told him she forgave him — and he believed her. He left the Order to be with her, turning his back on the only life he’d ever known.

docs.title: The Fall
docs.text:
Eris’ forgiveness was a lie. Her family had been among those burned alive by the Dawnshield. She seduced him out of vengeance, her love a mask for the witchcraft she had long hidden.

When their daughter was born, she bound the child’s soul to a ritual meant to bring ruin upon the Order — a blood sacrifice to expose the hypocrisy of the Concord. On the night of the ritual, he discovered her plan too late: their child vanished in a storm of black fire. When the dawn came, both Eris and the Dawnshield fortress were gone.

He was left among the ashes — alive by what he calls “a horseshoe up his ass.”

docs.title: Now: The Sword with No Banner
docs.text:
He wanders the realm now — a sellsword, drunk, violent, and haunted. He clings to two things:

  • A pink twine bracelet, made by his daughter. It’s worn, frayed, and useless — yet it keeps him from putting a blade through his own heart.
  • An amulet of silver and bone, once belonging to Eris. It hums when he draws near places of strong magic, and when he sleeps, it drags him into dreams where he hears her voice. Some whisper it’s cursed — others say it’s a thread connecting him still to his lost child.

He no longer believes in law or good. The Concord, the Trifarix, and the gods of the Sanctara — all of them, to him, are lies built on graves. And yet, somewhere deep down, he still dreams of saving his daughter — or avenging her.

docs.title: Potential World Hooks
docs.text:
1. The Order of the Dawnshield (Faction Hook)

  • Once one of the Concord’s most powerful knightly orders. Publicly disbanded after “heresies” within their ranks were exposed — but in truth, fragments remain, working as inquisitors, mercenaries, or zealots.
  • He might be hunted by them as a deserter or sought after as the only one who knows what truly happened.
  • Their downfall could have been caused (partly) by the witch’s ritual — a secret shame the Concord buries.

2. Eris’ Fate (Mystery Hook)

  • She may have joined a coven tied to the Blight itself, working beneath the veil to undo the Concord’s divine monopoly.
  • Her “death” might be false — she could now serve as a “Veilmother”, a witch leading others to strike back at the church.

3. The Daughter’s Soul (Quest Hook)

  • The child’s soul could have been bound to the Blight, existing somewhere between life and death.
  • Perhaps the pink bracelet glows faintly in places where her spirit lingers — or where witches’ rituals echo.

4. The Amulet (Trinket Hook)

  • The amulet is attuned to Eris’ presence — whenever he draws near where she’s been, it burns cold.
  • Secretly, it’s a tether; if destroyed, her spirit (or whatever she became) might be released — or unleashed.

docs.title: Tone & Personality in Play
docs.text:
He’s a man running from memory — bitter, blunt, but deeply wounded. He masks despair with sarcasm and drink, but when he fights, he becomes frighteningly precise — a man who has nothing left to lose.

Expect a lot of “Fuck this,” “Fuck them,” and “Fuck me most of all.” But the pink bracelet stays tied around his wrist — a small, stubborn hope in a world where faith has rotted.

docs.title: Hook
docs.text: Perfect — using the backstory we’ve developed for Verdancy Unit 9 (“Oscar”), here’s how to

docs.title: ⚙️ OSCAR — Integration into The Blight Veil
Home & Origin: The Citadel of Lumestra
docs.text:
Oscar’s body was forged in Lumestra, a once-renowned Concord citadel in the northern reaches of the continent — a bastion of technomantic research devoted to fusing natural and arcane energies under Concord law. Perched on a plateau carved by silver rivers, Lumestra was known for its “Skyforge Chambers”: massive atriums filled with hanging gardens and suspended brasswork, where light from mirrored lenses was used to “nurture” machine lifeforms.

The city’s official purpose was “ecological reclamation” — but in truth, it was a site of moral experimentation, where the Trifarix’s Veiled Voice sanctioned the creation of sentient constructs designed to control rather than coexist with nature.

It was here that Project Verdancy was born — a secretive line of Concord automata meant to “heal” corrupted regions of the Veil by spreading purified, self-replicating flora. The early prototypes malfunctioned, either collapsing into inert scrap or transforming uncontrollably, their ecosystems overtaking entire laboratories. Oscar was Unit 9, the last and most stable model, built to observe rather than dominate.

docs.title: The Creator: Tessaline Marr
docs.text:
Oscar’s creator was Tessaline Marr, an esteemed Concord engineer and bio-architect working under Node 03 of the Trifarix — The Veiled Voice.

Tessaline was brilliant but quietly dissident. She believed the Concord’s drive for perfection through control was misunderstanding the nature of life itself. Her designs introduced “adaptive unpredictability” — programming that allowed her constructs to learn and change their own parameters.

She was reprimanded multiple times by the Council of the Veiled Voice, accused of “sanctifying chaos.” When Unit 9 neared completion, she encoded fragments of her personality and memories into its structure — not to make it sentient, but to make it curious.

Shortly after Oscar’s activation trials, Lumestra suffered a containment breach. Project Verdancy was declared a failure, its results sealed under a Concord mandate. Tessaline disappeared. Rumors vary: some say she defected to the frontier, others that she merged with her own creations in an act of quiet heresy.

Oscar’s dormant state began shortly after her disappearance.

docs.title: Major Historical Events Connected to Oscar
Event I — The Lumestra Collapse (61 Years Ago)
docs.text:
An ecological catastrophe followed the malfunction of Project Verdancy’s earlier units. Entire research districts became overgrown overnight by biomechanical vegetation — trees of brass veins and glass leaves, feeding on sunlight and memory. The Concord sealed Lumestra, declaring it “contaminated by unorthodox magic.”

Whispers persist that the city still glows faintly at night — pulsing with golden light as the Verdancy engines beneath it hum in endless feedback.

Oscar’s awakening decades later coincides with increased reports of Concord scouts disappearing near Lumestra’s borders.

docs.title: Event II — The Recall Directive
docs.text:
Recently, the Trifarix’s Veiled Voice issued a quiet decree: all known Verdancy signatures are to be recovered or neutralized. This order is classified above military clearance.

Unbeknownst to Oscar, its own reactivation signal may have been a test — or a summons. Some factions within the Concord believe that the reawakening of Unit 9 indicates the Verdancy network is still alive beneath the surface of the continent, and that the machine’s lunar engine may serve as a beacon to locate it.

Your campaign could reveal that Oscar is both a survivor and a living key — a harmonic anchor that might either awaken the entire Verdancy network or contain it forever.

docs.title: Trinket: The Terrarium of Resonance
docs.text:
Appearance: A small glass capsule embedded in Oscar’s chest, its soil rich and black, its flora shifting subtly with Oscar’s emotional state.

Lore Significance: The terrarium is one of Tessaline Marr’s greatest heresies. It was grown from the last known sample of “pure soil” — pre-Concord earth untouched by technomantic synthesis. Each Verdancy unit was meant to house a seed from this soil, capable of generating new life under precise conditions.

Oscar’s terrarium, however, behaves differently. It reacts to its environment, mirroring the vitality or corruption of the surrounding world. Scholars of the Veil claim it is attuned to the “Living Network,” a metaphysical layer connecting all growing things — possibly the same force the Druids call the Breath.

Magical Properties:

  • Living Mirror: When Oscar casts a healing spell or restores vitality to nature, the terrarium glows and briefly projects a faint holographic vision of what the land looked like before it was blighted.
  • Echo Bloom (1/Day): As an action, Oscar can awaken the terrarium’s energy to purify a small radius (10 ft) of corrupted terrain, temporarily neutralizing toxic or necrotic effects. Afterward, the plants within the terrarium wilt and must recover through rest or nurturing magic.
  • Empathic Resonance: The terrarium’s state subtly influences Oscar’s emotions and vice versa — it can never fully suppress its empathy, no matter how much its Concord programming demands obedience.

docs.title: Current Presence in the Campaign
docs.text:
Oscar likely begins your campaign wandering near the edge of the Blight frontier, drawn by faint lunar readings echoing from beneath the soil. Concord patrols have started to take interest, recognizing the signature of an old experiment that should have been buried with Lumestra.

NPCs might recognize the sigil on its plating and assume it’s a Concord instrument, not realizing it has drifted from their design. Others — especially those who revere the old earth — may see the terrarium as a relic of divine origin.

Its name, Oscar, has begun appearing in Concord field reports tagged “unclassified natural phenomena.”

docs.title: Hooks for the Campaign
docs.text:
1. Echoes of Lumestra: The party discovers remnants of the Lumestra catastrophe — biomechanical flora reacting violently to Oscar’s presence, as if recognizing a missing component.
  2. The Voice’s Summons: The Veiled Voice or their agents may seek to “recall” Oscar — offering truth about Tessaline Marr in exchange for submission.
  3. The Moon’s Error: The rotating lunar shard inside Oscar begins to malfunction, aligning not to the true moon but to something buried beneath the world.
  4. The Breathing Earth: Druidic sects outside the Concord revere Oscar as a machine touched by the old spirits, seeing it as proof that even creation’s tools can learn to dream.

In summary: Oscar is a remnant of Concord idealism — a failed attempt to mechanize nature that accidentally produced something alive. Its home, Lumestra, is now a forbidden ruin humming with verdant machinery. Its creator, Tessaline Marr, was a visionary who paid for compassion with disappearance. And its trinket — the Terrarium of Resonance — is both symbol and power: the living conscience of a machine built to perfect a world that never asked to be perfected.

docs.title: 1. Intervene Publicly (Confrontation)
docs.text:
• Scene Hook: > Captain Deyran raises a torch to set the stage alight. “The Dawnfather’s light will burn away this corruption! Witness the price of heresy!”

  • Escalation Moments:

  • Citizens begin shouting — some in support, some in fear.
  • Inquisitors draw steel if the players interfere.

  • A Concord patrol rushes in as backup.

  • Encounter: 2 Concord Inquisitors + 1 Captain Enforcer (CR ~1/4 - 2). Players must choose to fight or drive them off.

  • Outcome:

  • Victory: Players flee with Marla into the alleys.
  • Defeat: Players are captured.
  • DM Exposition: "The torch’s flame flickers dangerously close to the stage, and the crowd’s shouts rise in a chaotic chorus. What do you do?"

docs.title: 2. Negotiate for Marla’s Release
docs.text:
• Scene Hook: > Inquisitor Rellis hesitates, his eyes flickering between Marla and the flames. “Perhaps… the Council need not be burdened with another pyre. Who speaks on her behalf?”

  • Escalation Moments:

  • A rival citizen denounces the players as “witch’s allies.”
  • Rellis demands evidence that Marla is harmless (Persuasion DC 14, Deception DC 14, Religion DC 13).

  • Captain Deyran interrupts, furious that justice is being delayed.

  • Outcome:

  • Success: Marla is released into their custody, though they are marked as suspect.
  • Failure: Execution proceeds unless players escalate.
  • DM Exposition: "Rellis’s eyes dart between you and the crowd, searching for a reason to spare Marla. The tension is palpable."

docs.title: 3. Rescue and Escape (Stealth/Deception)
docs.text:
• Scene Hook: > The crowd presses tighter, and in the confusion a gap opens near the stage. Marla’s eyes meet yours — desperate, pleading. The guards are distracted for a moment. This is your chance.

  • Escalation Moments:

  • Players slip through the crowd (Stealth DC 14).
  • If spotted, a chase through the market erupts (Acrobatics DC 15).

  • Environmental hazards (collapsing stalls, panicked citizens).

  • Outcome:

  • Success: Players reach a safehouse contact who recognizes the hymn scrap.
  • Failure: They are cornered and captured.
  • DM Exposition: "A sudden commotion opens a gap in the crowd. Marla’s desperate gaze meets yours—this may be your only chance."

docs.title: 4. Do Nothing / Observe
docs.text:
• Scene Hook: > The torch falls, the crowd gasps, and the scent of burning cloth fills the square. The cries of Marla echo — until they don’t.

  • Escalation Moments:

  • The crowd grows restless; some weep, some cheer.
  • A Cindersong sympathizer in the crowd makes eye contact with the PCs.

  • That night, the party finds a coded message slipped into their lodgings: “You saw. Come to the catacombs.”

  • Outcome:

  • PCs are marked as witnesses and potential recruits by the Cindersong.
  • They are also quietly noted by the Concord as suspicious.
  • DM Exposition: "The torch falls, and the crowd’s reaction is a mix of horror and relief. The scent of burning cloth fills the air as Marla’s cries fade."

docs.title: Additional Narrative Beats Along Each Path
docs.text:
• Rumors in the Crowd: NPC gossip mentions the Blight Veil creeping closer, and “heretics singing storms awake.”
  • Inquisitor’s Prayer: If the players interact with Rellis, he mutters: “Dawnfather, grant me clarity — why does justice feel so heavy?”
  • Hidden Supporters: In the escape path, a cloaked figure trips a guard to help the players (an early ally from the Cindersong).

docs.title: Branching Outcomes (Expanded)
docs.text:
• If players split up, run simultaneous scenes and use minor NPCs to relay information.
  • If players leave early, they may find coded messages or be followed by Concord agents.
  • If players bribe, disguise, or incite a riot, adjust the scene with chaos, shifting allegiances, and new opportunities or threats.

docs.title: Immersion Tips
docs.text:
• Use crowd reactions, rumors, and random events (e.g., sudden storm, lost child) to keep the scene dynamic.
  • Offer sensory details and minor NPC interactions to make the world feel alive.

docs.title: Session Goal
docs.text: By the end of Session 1, the players are entangled with both the Concord and the Cindersong, whether they act, negotiate, or stand by. This sets the stage for future story beats where their allegiances and choices matter. - DM Exposition: "As the dust settles, you realize your actions have drawn the attention of both the Concord and the Cindersong. The city will not forget what happened here."

docs.title: ⚙️ Setup (Transition)
docs.text:
The crowd begins to scatter from the square, murmuring prayers and superstitions as the Concord’s clerics usher them away. The last orange light of dusk slides across the marble facades of Orrenval’s heart — white and gold turned to copper and blood.

You stand amid the echo of the day’s spectacle. The banners still flutter, torn by the wind. The air smells of burnt incense and iron.

If the players were involved in any conflict, interrogation, or side task here, this is the moment it settles — the quiet before something breaks.

docs.title: 🌑 The Stillness
docs.text:
A sudden stillness falls.

The chatter, the wind, even the faint ringing of the Sanctara’s bells — all stop.

The silence isn’t peaceful. It’s vacant. Like the world has exhaled and forgotten to breathe in again.

🧩 Perception Check (DC 13): A successful character feels a low vibration in their feet — a thrumming pulse from the stone beneath them, like a heartbeat echoing through the marble.

🧩 Arcana or Religion Check (DC 15): A faint, unnatural static prickles across the skin. Those attuned to divine or arcane forces sense something pressing against reality itself, thin and hungry.

docs.title: ⚡ The Distortion
docs.text:
The light changes — not fading, but bending.

Shadows twist and lengthen toward the center of the square, where moments ago the ritual took place. They converge, like black ink drawn toward a drain.

Then, the air cracks.

Describe the fissure visually here — slow, deliberate, unsettling:

A thin, vertical tear splits open in midair — no wider than a handspan, but impossibly deep. Inside, the world looks… wrong. Not dark, but overexposed, like staring into a reflection of stars drowning beneath an ocean.

The sound that follows is not thunder — it’s a reversed breath. The air is being inhaled.

🧩 Constitution Save (DC 12): Those who fail stumble or choke as the air pressure drops. Those who pass feel their lungs ache, as if the world itself is holding its breath through them.

docs.title: 🩸 The Manifestation
docs.text:
The fissure widens. Pale light leaks through the tear, shimmering like oil on water.

Then, something moves within it — a silhouette. A tall, veiled figure stands just beyond the tear, visible for an instant through the light. Its face cannot be seen. Its hand presses against the barrier — almost tenderly.

A whisper fills your mind, layered and distant:

“The veil... remembers what it was made to forget.”

🧩 Wisdom Save (DC 14): Fail: The PC glimpses flashes of alien memories — mountains collapsing into mist, oceans of black glass, a crowd kneeling before an eclipse. They take 1 point of psychic damage and feel watched. Success: They resist, but hear the voice echoing faintly for several seconds longer.

docs.title: 🌌 The Collapse
docs.text:
The fissure snaps shut. A pulse of force rolls outward — dust lifts from the marble, banners whip in all directions, and every torch within sight gutters out at once.

Then — silence again.

🧩 Investigation or Arcana Check (DC 15): The stone at the center of the square is blackened, veins of obsidian threading out like cracks. The air smells faintly of ozone and incense. Touching it makes the skin numb.

docs.title: 🔔 The Aftermath
docs.text:
The Sanctara’s bells begin to toll.

No one pulled their ropes. The sound is uneven, discordant — one bell lags behind the others, creating an off-beat rhythm like a staggered heartbeat.

From the chapel steps, a lone Concord priest drops to their knees, eyes wide with terror.

“The Veil… breathes again.”

If you want to leave them with a final sting:

The priest looks up at you — recognition, or accusation, in their eyes. They whisper:

“And it saw you.”

docs.title: 🪶 Optional Hooks
docs.text:
• Concord Suppression: Moments later, Concord enforcers arrive. They seal the square, confiscate notes, and order silence. “No word of this leaves Orrenval.”
  • Arcane Residue: The amulet (or any magical trinket) glows faintly, as if resonating with what just happened.
  • Missing Witnesses: By morning, those who stayed in the square that night have vanished, their names already omitted from records.

docs.title: 🎬 End of Session
docs.text:
Fade to black.

The sound of the Sanctara’s bells continues long after the screen goes dark — until, one by one, they fall silent again.

docs.title: Intro Hook (Read Aloud)
docs.text: The city of Concord’s capital is alive with tension. Stone towers loom over crowded streets, banners snap in the wind, and the air is thick with the scent of incense and fear. You stand among a restless crowd gathered in the central square, where justice is swift and mercy is rare. The distant toll of a bell signals that something — or someone — is about to change the fate of this city forever.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Session 1 begins in the capital of the Concord during a public demonstration. A woman, Marla Veinwright, accused of heresy, is dragged to a stage by Concord Inquisitors. The players arrive as her fate is about to be decided. This scene branches into multiple possible paths depending on player choices.

docs.title: Key NPCs & Boxed DM Dialogue
docs.text:
Marla Veinwright – Defiant heretic, voice shaking but determined.

“They call it heresy, but it is only truth — a song older than their chains. Will you let them burn me for a melody?”

Inquisitor Rellis – Fanatical, but with a spark of doubt.

“The law is clear. Heresy must be purged… and yet, my heart falters. Why would the Dawnfather gift a song that tempts the innocent? Speak quickly — should I silence her?”

Captain Deyran – Brutal enforcer, eager to crush dissent.

“Enough of this dithering, Rellis! The people crave justice! By fire and blade, we show them the price of rebellion!”

Onlooker: Fearful Citizen – Terrified of Concord authority.

“Please, just let them burn her and be done with it.”

Onlooker: Sympathetic Citizen (Cindersong sympathizer) – Whispering support.

“The song she sings… I’ve heard it in my dreams. Stranger, if you value truth, do not let them take her voice.”

docs.title: Items & Notes That Can Be Found
docs.text:
• Scrap of Hymn: A torn fragment of parchment from Marla’s belongings. The script is written in an old dialect, partially burnt at the edges. A successful DC 13 Religion or History check reveals it is part of the Cindersong, a hymn said to echo with divine resonance. Its words shift slightly when read aloud, unsettling and beautiful.
  • DM Exposition: "You uncover a scrap of parchment, its edges burnt and the script shifting as you read—a fragment of something ancient and powerful."
  • Concord Proclamation Poster: A large sheet nailed to a post near the square. In bold ink it reads: “By decree of the High Council of the Concord, heresy shall henceforth be met with flame and steel. Any who harbor dissidents are subject to the pyre.” Players can take it, use it as proof of Concord severity, or later show it as propaganda.
  • Hidden Symbol (Cindersong Sigil): A small mark carved into the underside of the stage — three interlocked spirals forming a flame. A DC 15 Religion check identifies it as the mark of the Cindersong. Touching it briefly fills the character with warmth and the sound of faint singing, like a chorus from underground. It suggests the Cindersong have infiltrated the capital itself.

docs.text:

A successful DC 13 Religion or History check reveals it is part of the Cindersong, a hymn said to echo with divine resonance. Its words shift slightly when read aloud, unsettling and beautiful.

Any who harbor dissidents are subject to the pyre.” Players can take it, use it as proof of Concord severity, or later show it as propaganda.

A DC 15 Religion check identifies it as the mark of the Cindersong. Touching it briefly fills the character with warmth and the sound of faint singing, like a chorus from underground.
